name: Via Sancti Martini - Zaragoza Way
name_original: Azud de Urdán y Peña del Cuervo desde San Juan de Mozarrifar
description: Hike the Via Sancti Martini - Zaragoza Way, a moderate 9.6-mile route following ancient paths and historical sites near Zaragoza.
region_intro: Hiking around Zaragoza offers a diverse landscape, primarily shaped by the Ebro River and its surrounding plains. The region features a mix of riverine ecosystems, agricultural areas, and low-lying hills, providing varied terrain for outdoor activities. Pine forests and natural parks are also present, offering shaded paths and opportunities for wildlife observation. Best hiking trails around Zaragoza The most popular hiking route is Historic center of Zaragoza — circular from Av. de Cataluña, a 4.2 miles (6.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 47 minutes to complete. This easy route explores the city's historic core. Another top favourite among local hikers is Ebro Natural Trail - Aragón (GR 99), a moderate 6.3 miles (10.2 km) path. This trail follows the Ebro river, offering views of the waterway and its natural surroundings. Local hikers also love the Gardens of la Alfranca and Carrasco Pond — circular through the Pinares de la Alfranca, a 4.3 miles (6.9 km) trail leading through pine forests and past a pond, often completed in about 1 hour 44 minutes. Hiking around Zaragoza is defined by river paths, open plains, and pine woodlands. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy strolls to more challenging routes in the surrounding hills. The routes in Zaragoza are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 600 reviews. More than 3,700 hikers have used komoot to explore Zaragoza's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Zaragoza?
A: There are over 420 hiking routes around Zaragoza, offering a wide variety of options for all skill levels. The komoot community has rated these trails with an average score of 4.5 stars.

Q: Are there easy hiking trails suitable for beginners in Zaragoza?
A: Yes, Zaragoza offers many easy trails perfect for beginners. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974939209/gardens-of-la-alfranca-and-carrasco-pond-circular-through-the-pinares-de-la-alfranca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gardens of la Alfranca and Carrasco Pond — circular through the Pinares de la Alfranca&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 4.3-mile (6.9 km) route through pine forests. Another accessible option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974295579/almonacid-de-la-cuba-belchite-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almonacid de la Cuba-Belchite Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which is also considered easy.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Zaragoza?
A: The best times for hiking in Zaragoza are generally spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, temperatures are mild and pleasant, making for comfortable conditions. Summers can be very hot, especially in the Ebro plains, so early mornings or late afternoons are recommended if hiking then. Winters are generally mild, but some areas might be cooler.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking routes in Zaragoza?
A: Many trails around Zaragoza are suitable for families. The region features river paths, open plains, and pine woodlands, providing varied terrain. Easy routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974939209/gardens-of-la-alfranca-and-carrasco-pond-circular-through-the-pinares-de-la-alfranca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gardens of la Alfranca and Carrasco Pond — circular through the Pinares de la Alfranca&lt;/a&gt; are great for a leisurely family outing.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes near Zaragoza?
A: Yes, there are several excellent circular routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979614617/toro-de-osborne-and-castillo-de-cornel-circular-through-the-montes-blancos-de-alfajarin" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Toro de Osborne and Castillo de Cornel — circular through the Montes Blancos de Alfajarín&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 6.4 km loop offering scenic views. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e980025102/ebro-natural-trail-loop-from-el-burgo-de-ebro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ebro Natural Trail Loop from El Burgo de Ebro&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 13.9 km trail along the river.

Q: Are there any trails that offer viewpoints or interesting landmarks?
A: Absolutely. Many trails provide opportunities to see interesting sights. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e979614617/toro-de-osborne-and-castillo-de-cornel-circular-through-the-montes-blancos-de-alfajarin" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Toro de Osborne and Castillo de Cornel — circular through the Montes Blancos de Alfajarín&lt;/a&gt; offers views of the iconic Toro de Osborne. You can also explore natural monuments like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5302773" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojo de las Almunias&lt;/a&gt; or enjoy panoramic views from the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5312123" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bujal Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;, both accessible via trails in the gorges around Zaragoza.

Q: What kind of natural attractions can I expect to see along the trails?
A: Zaragoza's hiking trails showcase diverse natural attractions. You can find riverine ecosystems along the Ebro, explore pine forests, and discover unique geological formations like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2834036" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Foz de Zafrane&lt;/a&gt; gorge. There are also several lakes, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5238060" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Balsa de Larralde&lt;/a&gt;, and salt flats like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5146890" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Salada de Mediana Salt Flat&lt;/a&gt;, offering varied landscapes.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers?
A: Yes, for those seeking a greater challenge, Zaragoza offers more difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e974886865/barranco-de-las-almunias-circular-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Barranco de las Almunias Circular Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 18.6 km route with significant elevation gain, providing a rewarding experience for experienced hikers.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Zaragoza?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 600 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse landscapes, from river paths and open plains to quiet pine woodlands, and the variety of routes suitable for different ability levels.

Q: Can I find trails that lead to caves or unique geological features?
A: Yes, you can explore trails that lead to interesting geological sites. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e980046227/caves-of-san-caprasio-from-the-hermitage-of-the-virgin-of-sabina-circular-through-the-sierra-de-alcubierre" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Caves of San Caprasio from the Hermitage of the Virgin of Sabina — circular through the Sierra de Alcubierre&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that takes you through the Sierra de Alcubierre to these fascinating caves.

Q: Are there trails suitable for hiking in winter?
A: Zaragoza generally experiences mild winters, making many trails accessible year-round. While some higher elevation areas might see occasional snow, most river paths and lower-lying routes remain suitable for winter hiking. Always check local weather conditions before heading out.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the hiking trails around Zaragoza?
A: Many trails in the Zaragoza region are dog-friendly, especially those in natural parks and rural areas. However, it's always advisable to check specific regulations for each trail or protected area, as some may require dogs to be on a leash or restrict access in certain zones. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and clean up after them.
tips:
Going along the banks of the Gállego River, we will arrive at the Urdán Dam, which is the origin of the homonymous ditch that was built in the 12th or 12th century and is possibly of Arab origin. We will find a ceiling with more information and some wooden tables in a leisure area.
It is advisable to visit it when the flow of the Gállego river is very high, as it will be much more beautiful.

Place of great natural beauty, with the river Gállego forming a kind of natural meanders that leave us a beautiful picture.
It is a busy place in the summer months.

Nice wooden footbridge over the river Gállego that is on the outskirts of the neighborhood of San Juan de Mozarrifar and that gives us some beautiful views.
On both sides, we have areas for children and/or picnic areas.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Via Sancti Martini - Zaragoza Way?
faqs.answer: The route primarily consists of rural roads, footpaths, and historic Roman roads. You can expect varied surfaces, with some sections potentially being well-established ancient paths.

faqs.question: What historical and natural sights can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: This segment of the Via Sancti Martini is rich in historical and cultural points of interest. You'll follow ancient Roman paths and pass through towns significant to Saint Martin. Notable highlights include the Azud de Urdán, the scenic Río Gállego Meanders, and the San Juan de Mozarrifar Footbridge. Starting in Zaragoza, you'll also find landmarks like the Basilica of Our Lady of El Pilar and La Seo Cathedral.

faqs.question: Is the Via Sancti Martini - Zaragoza Way suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: While this specific segment is about 15.4 km with minimal elevation, the overall Via Sancti Martini pilgrimage is considered moderate to challenging, requiring sustained daily walking. If you're new to long-distance hiking, it's wise to build up your fitness. This particular section is a good introduction to the longer pilgrimage.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: Given the potential for warm Spanish weather, spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) are generally the most pleasant times to hike. These seasons offer milder temperatures, making the journey more comfortable for sustained walking.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the trail?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are welcome on rural roads and footpaths in Spain, but it's always best to keep them on a leash, especially when passing through populated areas or near livestock. Be sure to carry water for your dog, as sources might be limited.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for the Via Sancti Martini - Zaragoza Way?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to hike this segment of the Via Sancti Martini. It's a public route, accessible to all.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The route begins in Zaragoza, specifically from San Juan de Mozarrifar. For parking, you'll typically find options in or near the towns along the route. Public transport to San Juan de Mozarrifar from Zaragoza city center is also an option.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several official trails. You'll find yourself on parts of the Via Sancti Martini - Chemin de Saragosse (72% of the route), as well as segments of the Colada del Merzalar, Camino del Escobar, and Pasarela de San Juan.

faqs.question: What should I pack for a hike on this trail?
faqs.answer: For a day hike, bring plenty of water, snacks, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and comfortable hiking shoes. Given the historical nature of the route, a camera is also a good idea. If you plan to extend your journey, consider lightweight layers and basic first-aid supplies.

faqs.question: Are there amenities like cafes or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: As the trail passes through towns and villages, you will likely find opportunities for cafes, restaurants, and accommodation, especially in larger centers like Zaragoza. However, services may be sparse in more rural sections, so plan accordingly and carry enough supplies.
